entries.name: CurDir
entries.description:
The constant character used by the operating system to refer to the current directory.

For example: '.' for POSIX or ':' for the classic Macintosh.

entries.name: ParDir
entries.description:
The constant string used by the operating system to refer to the parent directory.

For example: ".." for POSIX or "::" for the classic Macintosh.

entries.name: DirSep
entries.description: The character used by the operating system to separate pathname components, for example: '/' for POSIX, ':' for the classic Macintosh, and '\\' on Windows.

entries.name: AltSep
entries.description: An alternative character used by the operating system to separate pathname components, or the same as DirSep if only one separator character exists. This is set to '/' on Windows systems where DirSep is a backslash ('\\').

entries.name: PathSep
entries.description: The character conventionally used by the operating system to separate search path components (as in PATH), such as ':' for POSIX or ';' for Windows.

entries.name: FileSystemCaseSensitive
entries.description: True if the file system is case sensitive, false otherwise. Used by cmpPaths to compare filenames properly.

entries.name: ExeExt
entries.description: The file extension of native executables. For example: "" for POSIX, "exe" on Windows (without a dot).

entries.name: ScriptExt
entries.description: The file extension of a script file. For example: "" for POSIX, "bat" on Windows.

entries.name: DynlibFormat
entries.description: The format string to turn a filename into a DLL file (also called shared object on some operating systems).

entries.name: ExtSep
entries.description: The character which separates the base filename from the extension; for example, the '.' in os.nim.
